texts.orig: FIDELITY-IMPOSED DISPLACEMENT EDITING FOR THE LEARN2REG 2024 SHG-BF CHALLENGE
texts.text: FIDELITY-IMPOSED DISPLACEMENT EDITING FOR THE LEARN2REG 2024 SHG-BF CHALLENGE

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Jiacheng Wang ⋆ , Xiang Chen † , Renjiu Hu ‡ , Rongguang Wang § , Jiazheng Wang † , Min Liu † , Yaonan Wang † , Hang Zhang ‡∗

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: ABSTRACT
texts.text: ABSTRACT

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Co-examination of second-harmonic generation (SHG) and bright-field (BF) microscopy enables the differentiation of tissue components and collagen fibers, aiding the analysis of human breast and pancreatic cancer tissues. However, large discrepancies between SHG and BF images pose challenges for current learning-based registration models in aligning SHG to BF. In this paper, we propose a novel multi-modal registration framework that employs fidelity-imposed displacement editing to address these challenges. The framework integrates batch-wise contrastive learning, feature-based pre-alignment, and instance-level optimization. Experimental results from the Learn2Reg COMULISglobe SHG-BF Challenge validate the effectiveness of our method, securing the 1st place on the online leaderboard. Our code is available at https: //github.com/JackyWang22/Neon_ShgBf .
texts.text: Co-examination of second-harmonic generation (SHG) and bright-field (BF) microscopy enables the differentiation of tissue components and collagen fibers, aiding the analysis of human breast and pancreatic cancer tissues. However, large discrepancies between SHG and BF images pose challenges for current learning-based registration models in aligning SHG to BF. In this paper, we propose a novel multi-modal registration framework that employs fidelity-imposed displacement editing to address these challenges. The framework integrates batch-wise contrastive learning, feature-based pre-alignment, and instance-level optimization. Experimental results from the Learn2Reg COMULISglobe SHG-BF Challenge validate the effectiveness of our method, securing the 1st place on the online leaderboard. Our code is available at https: //github.com/JackyWang22/Neon_ShgBf .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Index Terms -Second-harmonic generation, Image registration, Contrastive learning.
texts.text: Index Terms -Second-harmonic generation, Image registration, Contrastive learning.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. INTRODUCTION
texts.text: 1. INTRODUCTION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Image registration is a fundamental task in medical imaging, crucial for aligning images from different modalities or time points. Second-harmonic generation (SHG) microscopy provides high-resolution images sensitive to collagen fibers, while bright-field (BF) microscopy with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining highlights various tissue components [1, 2]. Accurate registration of SHG and BF images is essential for comprehensive cancer tissue analysis, offering deeper insights into tissue structure and pathology.
texts.text: Image registration is a fundamental task in medical imaging, crucial for aligning images from different modalities or time points. Second-harmonic generation (SHG) microscopy provides high-resolution images sensitive to collagen fibers, while bright-field (BF) microscopy with hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining highlights various tissue components [1, 2]. Accurate registration of SHG and BF images is essential for comprehensive cancer tissue analysis, offering deeper insights into tissue structure and pathology.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: SHG-BF registration presents two primary challenges. First, the two modalities differ significantly: SHG images emphasize collagen fibers, while BF images highlight stained tissue components, resulting in large visual discrepancies. Second, the sparse distribution of highlighted structures in SHG creates a severe foreground-background imbalance, making it difficult for learning-based methods, including convolutional neural networks [3, 4, 5, 6], vision transformers [7, 8], and keypoint-based methods [9], to perform effectively.
texts.text: SHG-BF registration presents two primary challenges. First, the two modalities differ significantly: SHG images emphasize collagen fibers, while BF images highlight stained tissue components, resulting in large visual discrepancies. Second, the sparse distribution of highlighted structures in SHG creates a severe foreground-background imbalance, making it difficult for learning-based methods, including convolutional neural networks [3, 4, 5, 6], vision transformers [7, 8], and keypoint-based methods [9], to perform effectively.

texts.orig: To address these challenges, we propose a novel SHG-BF multimodal registration method with the following key contributions:
texts.text: To address these challenges, we propose a novel SHG-BF multimodal registration method with the following key contributions:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Batch-wise contrastive loss (B-NCE) : We introduce a batch-wise noise contrastive estimation loss to effectively capture shared features between SHG and BF images.
texts.text: Batch-wise contrastive loss (B-NCE) : We introduce a batch-wise noise contrastive estimation loss to effectively capture shared features between SHG and BF images.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Feature-based prealignment and instance optimization : A prealignment step using descriptor matching is followed by instance-level optimization to refine the registration.
texts.text: Feature-based prealignment and instance optimization : A prealignment step using descriptor matching is followed by instance-level optimization to refine the registration.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. Flexible transition of similarity metrics : We combine local normalized cross-correlation (LNCC) and cross mutual information function (CMIF) as similarity metrics, balancing global and local alignment.
texts.text: Flexible transition of similarity metrics : We combine local normalized cross-correlation (LNCC) and cross mutual information function (CMIF) as similarity metrics, balancing global and local alignment.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The novel contrastive learning loss addresses the modality gap, while the instance optimization overcomes the foreground-background imbalance. Quantitative and qualitative results demonstrate considerable improvements in registration accuracy and robustness, earning us 1st place on the online leaderboard of the Learn2Reg COMULISglobe SHG-BF Challenge.
texts.text: The novel contrastive learning loss addresses the modality gap, while the instance optimization overcomes the foreground-background imbalance. Quantitative and qualitative results demonstrate considerable improvements in registration accuracy and robustness, earning us 1st place on the online leaderboard of the Learn2Reg COMULISglobe SHG-BF Challenge.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. RELATED WORK
texts.text: 2. RELATED WORK

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Classical methods. Traditional registration methods typically formulate the problem as an iterative energy optimization, employing similarity criteria such as LNCC, mean-squared error (MSE), or mutual information (MI). Approaches like ANTS [10] and ELASTIX [11] utilize gradient descent for optimization but are prone to local minima, particularly when displacements exceed feature scales. Discrete optimization techniques based on Markov Random Fields (MRF) [12] and local cost aggregation [13, 14] have been proposed to mitigate these issues.
texts.text: Classical methods. Traditional registration methods typically formulate the problem as an iterative energy optimization, employing similarity criteria such as LNCC, mean-squared error (MSE), or mutual information (MI). Approaches like ANTS [10] and ELASTIX [11] utilize gradient descent for optimization but are prone to local minima, particularly when displacements exceed feature scales. Discrete optimization techniques based on Markov Random Fields (MRF) [12] and local cost aggregation [13, 14] have been proposed to mitigate these issues.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Regression-Based Learning. Regression-based methods [15] train convolutional neural networks (ConvNets) to directly estimate the affine matrix. However, ConvNets lack inherent coordinate information, making affine matrix regression challenging. Vision transformers [7] and keypoint-based methods like KeyMorph [9] attempt to overcome these limitations by leveraging invariant feature representations, but still struggle with the high foreground-background imbalance.
texts.text: Regression-Based Learning. Regression-based methods [15] train convolutional neural networks (ConvNets) to directly estimate the affine matrix. However, ConvNets lack inherent coordinate information, making affine matrix regression challenging. Vision transformers [7] and keypoint-based methods like KeyMorph [9] attempt to overcome these limitations by leveraging invariant feature representations, but still struggle with the high foreground-background imbalance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Descriptor Matching Learning. Descriptor matching methods [16, 17, 18, 19] effectively address the high foregroundbackground imbalance by relying on feature descriptors and feature matching, utilizing both handcrafted and learningbased descriptors. Traditional RANSAC and modern graphand attention-based approaches [20] are commonly used for matching. However, these approaches lack a fidelity loss, leaving the output predictions unconstrained.
texts.text: Descriptor Matching Learning. Descriptor matching methods [16, 17, 18, 19] effectively address the high foregroundbackground imbalance by relying on feature descriptors and feature matching, utilizing both handcrafted and learningbased descriptors. Traditional RANSAC and modern graphand attention-based approaches [20] are commonly used for matching. However, these approaches lack a fidelity loss, leaving the output predictions unconstrained.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. METHODS
texts.text: 3. METHODS

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Our proposed registration framework, illustrated in Fig. 2, consists of two main stages: a feature-based prealignment using contrastive representation learning and a test-time instance-level optimization for final registration. Initially, we learn a shared representation between SHG and BF images using our specially designed Batch-wise Noise Contrastive Estimation (B-NCE) loss. This shared latent space simpli- fies the multimodal registration problem into a monomodal one, making alignment more tractable. During inference, we refine the registration using ConvexAdam optimization, leveraging both cross-mutual information function (CMIF) and local normalized cross-correlation (LNCC) as similarity metrics to achieve accurate alignment.
texts.text: Our proposed registration framework, illustrated in Fig. 2, consists of two main stages: a feature-based prealignment using contrastive representation learning and a test-time instance-level optimization for final registration. Initially, we learn a shared representation between SHG and BF images using our specially designed Batch-wise Noise Contrastive Estimation (B-NCE) loss. This shared latent space simpli- fies the multimodal registration problem into a monomodal one, making alignment more tractable. During inference, we refine the registration using ConvexAdam optimization, leveraging both cross-mutual information function (CMIF) and local normalized cross-correlation (LNCC) as similarity metrics to achieve accurate alignment.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Let I A , I B ∈ R 2 denote the fixed and moving images, respectively. Our goal is to construct affine transformations Φ( I A , I B ) for each image pair, mapping R 2 → R 2 . We aim to align the transformed moving image to the fixed image, such that Φ( I A , I B ) ⊙ I B ∼ I A where ⊙ denotes the spatial transformation of I B using Φ( I A , I B ) .
texts.text: Let I A , I B ∈ R 2 denote the fixed and moving images, respectively. Our goal is to construct affine transformations Φ( I A , I B ) for each image pair, mapping R 2 → R 2 . We aim to align the transformed moving image to the fixed image, such that Φ( I A , I B ) ⊙ I B ∼ I A where ⊙ denotes the spatial transformation of I B using Φ( I A , I B ) .

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Features prealignment with contrastive learning
texts.text: 3.1. Features prealignment with contrastive learning

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Due to the significant differences between SHG and BF images, direct registration is challenging. To address this, we map both images into a common latent space where shared features can be effectively captured. We achieve this using contrastive representation learning with a specially designed Batch-wise Noise Contrastive Estimation (B-NCE) loss.
texts.text: Due to the significant differences between SHG and BF images, direct registration is challenging. To address this, we map both images into a common latent space where shared features can be effectively captured. We achieve this using contrastive representation learning with a specially designed Batch-wise Noise Contrastive Estimation (B-NCE) loss.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Inspired by CoMIR [21], we employ two identical Dense Tiramisu networks [22], D θ , to extract feature representations from I A and I B : C A = D θ ( I A ) and C B = D θ ( I B ) , where C A , C B ∈ R H × W × C and C is the number of feature channels. And we demonstrated the trained features shown in Fig. 3 second row.
texts.text: Inspired by CoMIR [21], we employ two identical Dense Tiramisu networks [22], D θ , to extract feature representations from I A and I B : C A = D θ ( I A ) and C B = D θ ( I B ) , where C A , C B ∈ R H × W × C and C is the number of feature channels. And we demonstrated the trained features shown in Fig. 3 second row.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Batch-wise noise contrastive estimation (B-NCE) loss. Our B-NCE loss is designed to enhance the network's ability to capture shared features between modalities by focusing on position-level similarities across the batch dimension. We extract patches from C A and C B of size p × p , resulting in sets of patches { X k } and { Y k } for k = 1 , . . . , N , where N is the number of patches. To compute the loss, we consider the features at corresponding spatial locations across the batch:
texts.text: Batch-wise noise contrastive estimation (B-NCE) loss. Our B-NCE loss is designed to enhance the network's ability to capture shared features between modalities by focusing on position-level similarities across the batch dimension. We extract patches from C A and C B of size p × p , resulting in sets of patches { X k } and { Y k } for k = 1 , . . . , N , where N is the number of patches. To compute the loss, we consider the features at corresponding spatial locations across the batch:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: x i,j = { X k ( i, j ) } N k =1 , y i,j = { Y k ( i, j ) } N k =1 , (1)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where ( i, j ) indexes the spatial positions within each patch.
texts.text: where ( i, j ) indexes the spatial positions within each patch.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Wedefine the similarity between features using the cosine similarity:
texts.text: Wedefine the similarity between features using the cosine similarity:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: s ( x i,j , y i,j ) = x ⊤ i,j y i,j ∥ x i,j ∥∥ y i,j ∥ . (2)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The B-NCE loss for each spatial position is:
texts.text: The B-NCE loss for each spatial position is:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: L i,j B-NCE = -log exp( s ( x i,j , y i,j ) /τ ) ∑ N k =1 exp( s ( x i,j , z k ) /τ ) , (3)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where z k includes both positive and negative samples, and τ is a temperature parameter.
texts.text: where z k includes both positive and negative samples, and τ is a temperature parameter.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The total loss is averaged over all spatial positions:
texts.text: The total loss is averaged over all spatial positions:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: L B-NCE = 1 H p W p H p ∑ i =1 W p ∑ j =1 L i,j B-NCE , (4)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Feature-based prealignment. With the learned representations C A and C B , we perform feature-based prealignment. We detect keypoints and extract descriptors using a method inspired by XFeat [23], adapted to our context. We match features between C A and C B (Fig. 3 second row) to compute an initial affine transformation Φ init . We also demonstrated the matching keypoints in Fig. 3 in blue dots and connected with green lines.
texts.text: Feature-based prealignment. With the learned representations C A and C B , we perform feature-based prealignment. We detect keypoints and extract descriptors using a method inspired by XFeat [23], adapted to our context. We match features between C A and C B (Fig. 3 second row) to compute an initial affine transformation Φ init . We also demonstrated the matching keypoints in Fig. 3 in blue dots and connected with green lines.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Test-time Instance Optimization
texts.text: 3.2. Test-time Instance Optimization

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To address misalignment caused by local contrast noise in contrastive feature mapping, we adopt the core principles of the ConvexAdam method [24], enabling flexible instance optimization between affine and deformable transformations. Unlike the original ConvexAdam, which optimizes flow parameters using hand-crafted features, our approach leverages learned features from the preceding stage and employs a multi-resolution pyramid across scales of 1 , 1 2 , 1 4 , 1 8 , 1 16 . This hierarchical setup allows for efficient alignment at both large and fine scales.
texts.text: To address misalignment caused by local contrast noise in contrastive feature mapping, we adopt the core principles of the ConvexAdam method [24], enabling flexible instance optimization between affine and deformable transformations. Unlike the original ConvexAdam, which optimizes flow parameters using hand-crafted features, our approach leverages learned features from the preceding stage and employs a multi-resolution pyramid across scales of 1 , 1 2 , 1 4 , 1 8 , 1 16 . This hierarchical setup allows for efficient alignment at both large and fine scales.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Following the prealignment, we generate displacement fields using a convolutional network. Displacements are assigned by evaluating patch similarity costs and identifying minimal cost positions. Smooth regularization is then applied through Gaussian smoothing, as described in [25]. Our fidelity loss combines Local Normalized Cross-Correlation (LNCC), which handles local similarities and addresses the foreground-background imbalance, with Cross Mutual Information Function (CMIF), which considers global intensity relationships to bridge the modality gap.
texts.text: Following the prealignment, we generate displacement fields using a convolutional network. Displacements are assigned by evaluating patch similarity costs and identifying minimal cost positions. Smooth regularization is then applied through Gaussian smoothing, as described in [25]. Our fidelity loss combines Local Normalized Cross-Correlation (LNCC), which handles local similarities and addresses the foreground-background imbalance, with Cross Mutual Information Function (CMIF), which considers global intensity relationships to bridge the modality gap.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since the primary features in SHG images are sparsely distributed, we avoid using the deformation field directly. Instead, we sample displacements based on SHG intensity and perform least-squares analysis on these sparse displacements,
texts.text: Since the primary features in SHG images are sparsely distributed, we avoid using the deformation field directly. Instead, we sample displacements based on SHG intensity and perform least-squares analysis on these sparse displacements,

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3. Multimodal Similarities: Mutual Information
texts.text: 3.3. Multimodal Similarities: Mutual Information

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We compute mutual information between fixed image I A ⊆ X A and moving image I B ⊆ X B based on cross-mutual information function (CMIF) [26, 27]. Given images I A and I B intersecting on I AB and I AB = ∅ , we firstly cluster each image I A and I B into level sets A ∈ Z and B ∈ Z accordingly using K-means. For each level a and b from the level sets A and B , we compute the marginal and joint histogram entries separately, Then, based on the normalized histograms, we derive the marginal and joint Shannon entropies as follows,
texts.text: We compute mutual information between fixed image I A ⊆ X A and moving image I B ⊆ X B based on cross-mutual information function (CMIF) [26, 27]. Given images I A and I B intersecting on I AB and I AB = ∅ , we firstly cluster each image I A and I B into level sets A ∈ Z and B ∈ Z accordingly using K-means. For each level a and b from the level sets A and B , we compute the marginal and joint histogram entries separately, Then, based on the normalized histograms, we derive the marginal and joint Shannon entropies as follows,

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: H A = -∑ a ∈A I a A · I B N AB log I a A · I B N AB ; (5)

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: H B = -∑ b ∈B I A · I b B N AB log I A · I b B N AB ; (6)

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: H AB = -∑ a ∈A ∑ b ∈B I a A · I b B N AB log I a A · I b B N AB , (7)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where I a A and I b B represent images where the pixel values equal to level set value a and b respectively; N AB is the number of total pixels in both image I A and I B . Finally, we can compute the mutual information using MI ( I A , I B ) = H A + H B -H AB .
texts.text: where I a A and I b B represent images where the pixel values equal to level set value a and b respectively; N AB is the number of total pixels in both image I A and I B . Finally, we can compute the mutual information using MI ( I A , I B ) = H A + H B -H AB .

Reliable expert annotations provide landmarks for validation and testing.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.2. Data Preprocessing and Evaluation Metrics
texts.text: 4.2. Data Preprocessing and Evaluation Metrics

texts.label: text
texts.orig: For preprocessing, we applied stochastic intensity transformations using B´ ezier curves [28] and random affine augmentations, including scaling, rotation, and shearing, to enhance the robustness of our model to intensity variations and geometric distortions. During validation and testing, we omitted stochastic augmentations but maintained normalization and intensity adjustments to ensure consistency.
texts.text: For preprocessing, we applied stochastic intensity transformations using B´ ezier curves [28] and random affine augmentations, including scaling, rotation, and shearing, to enhance the robustness of our model to intensity variations and geometric distortions. During validation and testing, we omitted stochastic augmentations but maintained normalization and intensity adjustments to ensure consistency.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We used the Learn2Reg 2024 Challenge metrics, primarily Target Registration Error (TRE), to measure registration accuracy. TRE calculates the average distance between matched landmarks in the fixed and moving images-lower values mean better alignment. Validation results are from the challenge leaderboard.
texts.text: We used the Learn2Reg 2024 Challenge metrics, primarily Target Registration Error (TRE), to measure registration accuracy. TRE calculates the average distance between matched landmarks in the fixed and moving images-lower values mean better alignment. Validation results are from the challenge leaderboard.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.3. Quantitative Results
texts.text: 4.3. Quantitative Results

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Table 1 presents the performance of our method compared to top-performing methods from the challenge. Our approach achieved a mean TRE of 1.943 mm on the validation set, ranking first. Notably, our method demonstrated the lowest standard deviation (0.765 mm) among all submissions, indicating high robustness and consistency across different cases.
texts.text: Table 1 presents the performance of our method compared to top-performing methods from the challenge. Our approach achieved a mean TRE of 1.943 mm on the validation set, ranking first. Notably, our method demonstrated the lowest standard deviation (0.765 mm) among all submissions, indicating high robustness and consistency across different cases.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To evaluate the effectiveness of our proposed components, we conducted ablation studies summarized in Table 1 (second part). LNCC is less sensitive to local noise and effectively handled the sparse SHG features, reducing the TRE by approximately 60%. However, it is less capable of capturing global structure. In contrast, CMIF captures global structure better but is more sensitive to local noise introduced by the contrastive feature mapping, leading to worse results when

texts.text: used alone. By combining LNCC with CMIF in our fidelity loss, we balanced local robustness and global alignment, reducing the TRE from 2.361 to 1.943. This demonstrates that our fidelity-imposed displacement editing enhances registration accuracy by integrating the strengths of both metrics.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Additionally, we tested different iteration counts during(15, 30, and 50 iterations) using both CMIF and LNCC (Fig. 5). Our method consistently achieved low TRE values, with the best performance at 30 iterations (mean TREs of 2.759, 1.943, and 2.168, respectively). This indicates our approach's stability without extensive computation.
texts.text: Additionally, we tested different iteration counts during(15, 30, and 50 iterations) using both CMIF and LNCC (Fig. 5). Our method consistently achieved low TRE values, with the best performance at 30 iterations (mean TREs of 2.759, 1.943, and 2.168, respectively). This indicates our approach's stability without extensive computation.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.4. Qualitative Results
texts.text: 4.4. Qualitative Results

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two validation examples are presented in Fig. 4. Despite significant structural differences due to the modality gap, our method successfully aligned the moving BF images to the fixed SHG images. The overlaid warped BF images show main structures aligned with minimal misalignment, demonstrating effective multimodal registration.
texts.text: Two validation examples are presented in Fig. 4. Despite significant structural differences due to the modality gap, our method successfully aligned the moving BF images to the fixed SHG images. The overlaid warped BF images show main structures aligned with minimal misalignment, demonstrating effective multimodal registration.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 3 shows feature matching with our learned representations. Despite large modality discrepancies, the features are visually similar, enabling robust matching. The sufficient matching pairs confirm the effectiveness of B-NCE loss in bridging the modality gap for feature-based prealignment.
texts.text: Figure 3 shows feature matching with our learned representations. Despite large modality discrepancies, the features are visually similar, enabling robust matching. The sufficient matching pairs confirm the effectiveness of B-NCE loss in bridging the modality gap for feature-based prealignment.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 5. CONCLUSION
texts.text: 5. CONCLUSION

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In this paper, we propose a new affine registration framework that integrates a novel contrastive learning loss (B-NCE) with rich contextual information from modern neural networks inside fidelity-imposed iterative instance optimization. Our fidelity-imposed network editing framework overcomes modality gap between SHG and BF images, providing robust and accurate SHG-BF registration performance. Experimental results from the Learn2Reg COMULISglobe SHG-BF Challenge demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, earning the 1st place on the online leaderboard.
texts.text: In this paper, we propose a new affine registration framework that integrates a novel contrastive learning loss (B-NCE) with rich contextual information from modern neural networks inside fidelity-imposed iterative instance optimization. Our fidelity-imposed network editing framework overcomes modality gap between SHG and BF images, providing robust and accurate SHG-BF registration performance. Experimental results from the Learn2Reg COMULISglobe SHG-BF Challenge demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach, earning the 1st place on the online leaderboard.
